audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a conversation between the director of a childcare centre and a parent enrolling her child in the centre.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, welcome to the Early Learning Childcare Centre. How may I help you?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Hi, I spoke to you last week about enrolling my daughter to next year.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I'll just get some details from you. So you're her mother?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: And, can I have your name?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: It's Carol, Carol Smith.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: The parent's name is Carol Smith, so Smith has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning. Welcome to the Early Learning Childcare Centre. How may I help you?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Hi. I spoke to you last week about enrolling my daughter to next year.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Oh, yes. I'll just get some details from you. So, you're her mother?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: And, can I have your name?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: It's Carol, Carol Smith.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: And your daughter's name?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: It's Kate.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Now, we have several groups at the centre and we cater for children from three to five years old. How old is your daughter?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: She's three now but she turns four next month.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: I'll put four down because that's how old she'll be when she starts.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Fine, she's so excited about her birthday and coming to the centre.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: That's good to hear. And what's your address?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: It's 46 Wombat Road, that's WOMBAT. Woodside 4032.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: And what's the phone number?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: So, have you decided on the days you'd like to bring your daughter here?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: I'd prefer Monday and Wednesday if possible.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Mmm. I'll check, Monday's fine, but I think the centre is already full for Wednesday. Erm. Yes. Sorry. It seems to be a very popular day. We can offer you a Thursday or a Friday as well.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Oh dear. I suppose Thursday would be all right because she has swimming on Friday.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: OK, got that. Because a lot of parents work, we do offer flexible start and finish times. We are open from 7:30 in the morning until 6 o'clock at night. What time would you like your daughter to start?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: I need to get to work in the city by 9:00 so I'll drop her off at 8:30 . You're pretty close to the city here so that should give me plenty of time to get there.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: That's fine. Now, we also need to decide which group she'll be in. we have two different groups and they're divided up according to age. There's the green group. Which is for three- to four-year-olds. And then there's the red group which is for four- to five-year-olds.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: She's quite mature for her age and she can already write her name and read a little.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Well, I'll put her in the red group and we can always change her to the green one if there are any problems.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: That sounds fine.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: OK. Let's move on to meals. We can provide breakfast, lunch and dinner. As she's finishing pretty early, she won't need dinner, will you give her breakfast before she comes?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, she'll only need lunch.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, does she have any medical conditions we need to know about? Does she have asthma or any hearing problems for example?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: No. But she does need to wear glasses.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Oh, I'll make a note of that.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, she's pretty good about wearing them, she can't see much without them.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Right. OK. Now, I also need emergency contact details.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: So what sort of information do you need?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Just the name and number of a friend or family member we can contact in case we can't get hold of you at any time.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: OK. That'd better be my sister... Jenny Ball . That's BALL. Her phone number is 3346 7523.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Great. So she is the child's aunt?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: I'll make a note of that as well. Now, is there anything you'd like to ask?
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: What about payment? How much are the fees each term?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Well, for two days and the hours you've chosen, that will be $450 altogether.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: OK, and do I have to pay that now?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: No, we send out invoices once the children start at the centre. You can choose to pay at the end of each term or we do offer a slightly discounted rate if you pay every month.
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, I'll do that then. I find it easier to budget that way and I'm not used to the term dates just yet.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Good, it makes it a lot simpler for us as well. Well, that's everything. Would you like me to show you around the centre?...
audioscript.speaker: DIRECTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Childcare Information

body.title: Early Learning Childcare Centre Enrolment Form

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a woman called Alice Bussell talking on the radio about the Dolphin Conservation Trust, an organization which tries to protect dolphins.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today we're pleased to have on the show Alice Bussell from the Dolphin Conservation Trust.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Uh. Tell us about the trust, Alice.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Well, obviously, its purpose is to protect dolphins in seas all around the world.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It tries to raise people's awareness of the problems these marine creatures are suffering because of pollution and other threats.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It started 10 years ago, and it's one of the fastest growing animal charities in the country.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Although it's still fairly small compared with the big players in animal protection, we're particularly proud of the work we do in education.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Last year, we visited a huge number of schools in different parts of the country.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Going round to talk to children and young people, aged from 5 to 18.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: In fact, about 35% of our members are children.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: The charity uses its money to support campaigns, for example, for changes in fishing policy and so forth.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It hopes soon to be able to employ its first full-time biologist with dolphin expertise to monitor populations.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Of course, many people give their services on a voluntary basis, and we now have volunteers working in observation, office work, and other things.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I should also tell you about the award we won from the Charity Commission last year, for our work in education.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Although it's not meant an enormous amount of money for us, it has made our activities even more widely publicized and understood.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: In the long term, it may not bring in extra members, but we're hoping it'll have this effect.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Is it possible to see dolphins in UK waters?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Yes, in several locations, and we have a big project in the east part of Scotland.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: This has long been a haven for dolphins, because it has very little shipping.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: However, that may be about to change soon, because oil companies want to increase exploration there.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: We're campaigning against this, because although there'll be little pollution from oil,
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: exploration creates a lot of underwater noise.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: It means the dolphins can't rest and socialize.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: This is how I became interested in dolphin conservation in the first place.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I had never seen one, and I hadn't been particularly interested in them at school.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Then I came across this story about a family of dolphins who had to leave their home in the Moray Firth.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Because of the oil companies, and about a child who campaigned to save them.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I couldn't put the book down.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I was hooked.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I'm sure our listeners will want to find out what they can do to help.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: You mentioned the 'Adopt a Dolphin' scheme.
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Can you tell us about that?
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: Of course, people can choose one of our dolphins to sponsor.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: They receive a picture of it, and news updates.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I'd like to tell you about four, which are currently being adopted by our members.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Moondancer, Echo, Kiwi, and Samson.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Unfortunately, Echo is being rather elusive this year, and hasn't yet been sighted by our observers.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: But we remain optimistic that he'll be out there soon.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: All the others have been out in force.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Samson and Moondancer are often photographed together, but it's Kiwi who's our real character, as she seems to love coming up close for the cameras, and we've captured her on film hundreds of times.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: They all have their own personalities.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Moondancer is very elegant and curves out and into the water very smoothly, whereas Samson has a lot of energy.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: He's always leaping out of the water with great vigor.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: You'd probably expect him to be the youngest, he's not quite, that's Kiwi, but Samson's the latest of our dolphins to be chosen for the scheme.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Kiwi makes a lot of noise, so we can often pick her out straight away.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Echo and Moondancer are noisy too, but Moondancer is easy to find, because she has a particularly large fin on her back.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Which makes her easy to identify.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: So, yes, they're all very different.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Well, they sound a fascinating group...
audioscript.speaker: INTERVIEWER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: You'd probably expect him to be the youngest, he's not quite, that's Kiwi, but Samson's the latest of our dolphins to be chosen for the scheme.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Kiwi makes a lot of noise, so we can often pick her out straight away.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Echo and Moondancer are noisy too, but Moondancer is easy to find, because she has a particularly large fin on her back.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Which makes her easy to identify.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: So, yes, they're all very different.
utterances.speaker: ALICE

utterances.text: Well, they sound a fascinating group...
utterances.speaker: INTERVIEWER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO things does Alice say about the Dolphin Conservation Trust?
options: Children make up most of the membership.
It's the country's largest conservation organisation.
It helps finance campaigns for changes in fishing practices.
It employs several dolphin experts full-time.

items.title: Why is Alice so pleased the Trust has won the Charity Commission award?
options: It has brought in extra money.
It made the work of the trust better known.

items.title: Alice says oil exploration causes problems to dolphins because of
Alice became interested in dolphins when
options: she saw one swimming near her home.
she heard a speaker at her school.
she read a book about them.

text: Which dolphin does Alice make each of the following comments about?
textReadable: Which dolphin does Alice make each of the following comments about?

items: It has not been seen this year.          <input>
It is photographed more than the others. <input>
It is the newest one in the scheme.      <input>
body.title: Comments

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a student called Rob, who is in the first year of a theatre studies course, talking to another student called Mia,
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: who's in the fourth year of the same course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Rob.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: How's the course going?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, Mia.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yeah, great.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I can't believe the first term's nearly over.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I saw your group's performance last night at the student theater.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: It was good.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Really.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yeah, but now we have to write a report on the whole thing, an in-depth analysis.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I don't know where to start.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Like I have to write about the role I played, the doctor, how I developed the character.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Well, what was your starting point?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Uh. My grandfather was a doctor before he retired, and I just based it on him.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: OK, but how?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Uh. Did you talk to him about it?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: He must have all sorts of stories, but he never says much about his work, even now.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: He has a sort of authority though.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Hmm, so how did you manage to capture that?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: I'd visualize what he must have been like in the past when he was sitting in his consulting room, listening to his patients.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: OK, so that's what you explain in your report.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Right, then there's the issue of atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: So in the first scene, we needed to know how boring life was in the doctor's village in the 1950s.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: So when the curtain went up on the first scene in the waiting room, there was that long silence before anyone spoke.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: And then people kept saying the same thing over and over like "Cold, isn't it?"
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Yes, and everyone wore grey and brown, and just sat in a row.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes, all those details of the production.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: And I have to analyze how I functioned in the group, what I found out about myself.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I know I was so frustrated at times when we couldn't agree.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Hmm. Yes, so did one person emerge as the leader?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Sophia did.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: That was OK.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: She helped us work out exactly what to do for the production, and that made me feel better, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: When you understood what needed doing.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And Sophia did some research too.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: That was useful in developing our approach.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Like what?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Well, she found these articles from the 1950s about how relationships between children and their parents, or between the public and people like bank managers or the police were shifting.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Interesting, and did you have any practical problems to overcome?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Well, in the final rehearsal, everything was going fine until the last scene - that's where the doctor's first patient appears on stage on his own.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The one in the wheelchair?
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And he had this really long speech with the stage all dark except for one spotlight.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And then that stuck somehow, so it was shining on the wrong side of the stage.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: But anyway, we got that fixed, thank goodness.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes, it was fine on the night.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: But while you're here, Mia, I wanted to ask you about the year abroad option.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Would you recommend doing that?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes, definitely.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: It's a fantastic chance to study in another country for a year.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: I think I'd like to do it, but it looks very competitive.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: There's only a limited number of places.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes, so, next year when you're in the second year of the course, you need to work really hard in all your theater studies modules.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Only students with good marks get places.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: You have to prove that you know your subject really well.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So how did you choose where to go?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Well, I decided I wanted a program that would fit in with what I wanted to do after I graduate.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: So, I looked for a university with emphasis on acting, rather than directing, for example.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: It depends on you.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Then about 6 months before you go, you have to email the scheme coordinator with your top 3 choices.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: I had a friend who missed the deadline and didn't get her first choice, so you do need to get a move on at that stage.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: You'll find that certain places are very popular with everyone.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: And don't you have to write a personal statement at that stage?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I'll get some of the final year students to give me some tips, maybe see if I can read what they wrote.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I think that's a very good idea.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: I don't mind showing you what I did, and while you're abroad, don't make the mistake I made.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: I got so involved, I forgot all about making arrangements for when I came back here for the final year.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Make sure you stay in touch so they know your choices for the optional modules.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: You don't want to miss out doing your preferred specialisms.
audioscript.speaker: MIA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear part of a lecture about leadership, introducing a theory called self-regulatory focus theory.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today, I want to talk about self-regulatory focus theory, and how the actions of leaders can affect the way followers approach different situations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Self-regulatory focus theory is a theory developed by Tori Higgins.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He says that a person's focus at any given time is to either approach pleasure or avoid pain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are two basic motivations that each and every one of us has, and they cause us to have different kinds of goals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Promotion goals in different life situations emphasize achievement.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Prevention goals are oriented towards the avoidance of punishment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In a specific situation, our thoughts might focus more on promotion goals, or more on prevention goals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The theory suggests that two factors affect which goals we are focusing on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First, there is a chronic factor.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This factor is connected to a person's personality, and says that each person has a basic tendency to either focus more on promotion goals or focus more on prevention goals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As part of his or her personality.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Second, there is a situational factor, which means that the context we are in can make us more likely to focus on one set of goals or the other.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, we are more likely to be thinking about pleasure and to have promotion goals when we are spending time with a friend.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In contrast, if we are working on an important project for our boss, we are more likely to try to avoid making mistakes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And therefore have more prevention goals in our mind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Research has shown that the goals we are focusing on at a given time affect the way we think, for example, when focusing on promotion goals, people consider their ideal self.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Their aspirations and gains.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They don't think about what they can lose, so they think in a happier mode, they feel more inspired.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To change.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When people are focusing on prevention goals, they think about their ought self, what are they supposed to be?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What are people expecting from them?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They consider their obligations to others.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As a result, they experience more anxiety and try to avoid situations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Where they could lose.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now that I have talked about the two focuses, and how they affect people, I want to look at the idea that the way leaders behave.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or their style of leading can affect the focus that followers adopt in a specific situation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In talking about leadership, we often mention transformational leaders and transactional leaders.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Transformational leaders when interacting with their followers, focus on their development, in their words and actions, transformational leaders highlight change.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Their speech is passionate and conveys a definitive vision.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All of these things can encourage followers to think about what could be.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In other words, they inspire a promotion focus in their followers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In contrast, transactional leaders focus on developing clear structures that tell their followers exactly what is expected of them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While they do explain the rewards people will get for following orders, they emphasize more how a follower will be punished.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or that a follower won't get rewarded, if his or her behavior doesn't change.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In short, they emphasize the consequences of making a mistake.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This emphasis will clearly lead followers to focus on avoiding punishment and problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is clearly a prevention focus.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In conclusion, it is important to understand that one focus is not necessarily better than the other one.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For a designer who works in a field where a lot of innovation is needed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A promotion focus is probably better.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In contrast, a prevention focus, which causes people to work more cautiously, and produce higher quality work,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: might be very appropriate for a job like a surgeon, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main point of the research though, is that the actions of leaders can greatly influence whether people approach a situation with more of a promotion focus or more of a prevention focus.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: For example, we are more likely to be thinking about pleasure and to have promotion goals when we are spending time with a friend.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In contrast, if we are working on an important project for our boss, we are more likely to try to avoid making mistakes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And therefore have more prevention goals in our mind.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Research has shown that the goals we are focusing on at a given time affect the way we think, for example, when focusing on promotion goals, people consider their ideal self.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Their aspirations and gains.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They don't think about what they can lose, so they think in a happier mode, they feel more inspired.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To change.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When people are focusing on prevention goals, they think about their ought self, what are they supposed to be?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What are people expecting from them?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They consider their obligations to others.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As a result, they experience more anxiety and try to avoid situations.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Where they could lose.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now that I have talked about the two focuses, and how they affect people, I want to look at the idea that the way leaders behave.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or their style of leading can affect the focus that followers adopt in a specific situation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In talking about leadership, we often mention transformational leaders and transactional leaders.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Transformational leaders when interacting with their followers, focus on their development, in their words and actions, transformational leaders highlight change.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Their speech is passionate and conveys a definitive vision.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All of these things can encourage followers to think about what could be.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In other words, they inspire a promotion focus in their followers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In contrast, transactional leaders focus on developing clear structures that tell their followers exactly what is expected of them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: While they do explain the rewards people will get for following orders, they emphasize more how a follower will be punished.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or that a follower won't get rewarded, if his or her behavior doesn't change.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In short, they emphasize the consequences of making a mistake.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This emphasis will clearly lead followers to focus on avoiding punishment and problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is clearly a prevention focus.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In conclusion, it is important to understand that one focus is not necessarily better than the other one.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For a designer who works in a field where a lot of innovation is needed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A promotion focus is probably better.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In contrast, a prevention focus, which causes people to work more cautiously, and produce higher quality work,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: might be very appropriate for a job like a surgeon, for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The main point of the research though, is that the actions of leaders can greatly influence whether people approach a situation with more of a promotion focus or more of a prevention focus.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: People's focus is to approach pleasure or avoid pain
items.title: Self-regulatory focus theory

items: we are more likely to focus on promotion goals when with a <input>
we are more likely to focus on prevention goals with our boss
Promotion Focus: People think about an ideal version of themselves, their <input> and their gains.
Prevention Focus: People think about their 'ought' self and their obligations
items.title: How people's focus affects them

items: Leadership behaviour and <input> affects people's focus
pay special attention to the <input> of their followers
emphasise the results of a mistake

items.title: Leaders

items: Promotion Focus is good for jobs requiring <input>
Prevention Focus is good for work such as a surgeon
Leaders' actions affect which focus people use
items.title: Conclusion
